sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Gregory, presented with a complaint of right knee pain, specifically on the outside of the knee, which started approximately 2.5 weeks ago after stepping off a curb.
He reports that he did not fall, but rather twisted his ankle and subsequently experienced pain in the knee.
The pain is described as clicking, which worsens with squatting or standing on one foot for an extended period.
He denies pain at rest or with palpation of the outside of the knee.
He reports some numbness and tingling in the foot, which is not new and unrelated to the current injury.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of breaking his second toe on his right foot three times over the last 10 years, resulting in numbness and tingling in the foot, which is not related to the current injury.
Review of systems: The patient denies any additional symptoms in other organs that are relevant to the initial presentation.
Current medications: The patient reports taking ibuprofen occasionally for pain, but not regularly.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has a slight effusion on the right knee, and a positive varus test.
The range of motion is full, and the Lachman sign is negative.
The patient has palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibialis pulses.
Test Results: The patient had an x-ray of the knee, which showed no acute fracture or bony abnormality.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a lateral collateral ligament strain, which can be healed on its own.
Problem list: Lateral collateral ligament strain; status: confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 15 mg once a day for pain management.
I referred the patient to physical therapy to strengthen the muscles and areas around the injury and prevent future injuries.
I advised the patient to continue icing the knee, with a 20-minute on, 20-minute off schedule, at least 4 times a day.
Follow-up: The patient will be referred to physical therapy.
If symptoms persist, an MRI may be considered.
